summary:
Neonate fungal infections commonly stem from maternal-to-newborn transfer via C. albicans during delivery; however, considering caregiver-hand transmission specifically, studies highlight higher incidence rates associated with C. albicans over others like C. glabrata, C. tropicalis, and C. parapsilosis.
The primary cause of neonatal fungal infections acquired through caregiver’s hands appears to be *Candida albicans*, given it accounts for about one-third of cases and has well-documented early-onset characteristics making it more prevalent than less frequent pathogens when direct skin-to-skin contact occurs between infected carriers and infants.

The primary cause of neonatal fungal infections acquired through caregiver’s hands appears to be *Candida albicans*, given it accounts for about one-third of cases and has well-documented early-onset characteristics making it more prevalent than less frequent pathogens when direct skin-to-skin contact occurs between infected carriers and infants.. Answer choice: A
